nativeRange.habitat: Coral reefs and rubble zones of shallow protected reefs, occasionally in more exposed areas; found at depths generally between 5 and 28 meters

diet.notes: Generally advised to offer a varied diet of vitamin-enriched frozen mysis shrimp, frozen brine shrimp, and other meaty marine foods. Has been known to accept high-quality marine flake and pellet foods once acclimated. Tends to feed multiple times per day in small portions. May occasionally consume small ornamental crustaceans.

avoidWith: small ornamental shrimp (may be viewed as prey)

summary: A peaceful and colorful wrasse from the Western Pacific, generally considered reef-safe and suitable for community reef aquariums. Males tend to display more vibrant coloration than females, and a tight-fitting lid is strongly advised as this species has been known to jump.
careNotes:
Fine Spotted Fairy Wrasse (Cirrhilabrus punctatus) is generally considered a good choice for a peaceful reef aquarium. Males tend to display more vivid coloration—typically red, pink, yellow, and blue hues with fine white spotting—while females and juveniles are somewhat duller with a dark gray to reddish upper body and white lower section.

This species has been reported to jump readily, and a tight-fitting canopy is strongly advised. A sand or rubble substrate with ample live rock for hiding and territories tends to work well.

Like other Cirrhilabrus wrasses, this species is a protogynous hermaphrodite: individuals are born female and the dominant fish in a group may transition to male. In a group setting, one male will typically pair or spawn with multiple females; keeping a single male with one or more females is feasible in appropriately sized systems.

Temperature sources showed moderate disagreement: LiveAquaria reports 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), FishBase reports 24–28°C, and SaltwaterFish.com reports 75–82°F (23.9–27.8°C). The range used here (23.4–27.1°C) is an average across all three sources. Tank size sources also differed: LiveAquaria recommends 90 gal (341 L) while SaltwaterFish.com recommends 50 gal (189 L); 265 L is used as the average. Care level differed between sources (easy vs moderate); intermediate is used as the averaged ordinal.

Care level also has a nuance: the baseline ease for this species is relatively beginner-friendly regarding diet and temperament, but the jumping risk and need for stable water quality push care toward intermediate overall.
breedingNotes: Cirrhilabrus punctatus is a protogynous hermaphrodite, with fish born female and the dominant individual capable of transitioning to male. Distinct pairing behavior during breeding has been observed in the wild. Captive breeding of Cirrhilabrus wrasses has been reported occasionally but is generally considered difficult and is not routine in the hobby.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (13.0 cm max), native range (Western Pacific: New South Wales to Fiji and Tonga), and temperature range (24–28°C). Temperature higher than LiveAquaria; averaged across sources.

additional.name: SaltwaterFish.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (5 in = 12.7 cm), temperature (75–82°F = 23.9–27.8°C), salinity (1.023–1.025), pH (8.1–8.4), tank size (50 gal = 189 L, lower than LiveAquaria). Tank size and temperature averaged across sources. Care level listed as moderate vs LiveAquaria easy; intermediate used as average.

fish.breedingNotes: Protogynous hermaphrodite; captive breeding is occasionally reported but not routine in the hobby. Distinct pairing during spawning has been observed in the wild.
